skill.name: yescan-scan-universal
skill.description: Enhances and cleans single image inputs using Quark Scan King. It can improve document photos, remove visual noise such as shadows or moire patterns, crop and rectify images, and save the processed result locally.
skill.summary: Enhance, clean, crop, and rectify single images through the Quark Scan King API.
skill.author: yescan-ai

security_audit.summary: AI review confirms the skill intentionally uploads user-provided images and the configured API key to the documented Quark Scan King endpoint, then writes processed images to a temp directory. The numerous command-execution, weak-crypto, Windows SAM, and obfuscation alerts are mostly false positives from markdown command examples, file path text, magic-byte checks, and base64 image handling. Publish only with a clear warning that images and credentials leave the local machine.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Image Upload
medium_findings.description: The skill sends user-supplied image data or image URLs to the Quark Scan King service. This is required for the skill purpose and disclosed, but it is a real privacy risk for sensitive documents, IDs, contracts, medical images, or confidential screenshots.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and POST request are explicit in code, and the security documents confirm user images leave the machine. The behavior is intentional rather than hidden, which lowers severity from high to medium.

medium_findings.title: API Key Sent to External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill reads SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY from the environment or ~/.yescan_env and places it in the request payload sent to the external API. This is normal authentication behavior, but it exposes a credential to the third-party service and requires careful key storage and rotation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential loading and inclusion in the outbound request are directly visible in code. The repository documents the behavior and mitigation, so it is a managed but real risk.

medium_findings.title: Local Image Files Are Read and Uploaded
medium_findings.description: When a local path is supplied, the script validates extension and size, reads the file, base64-encodes it, and submits it to the API. This is expected behavior, but users must avoid passing unintended sensitive files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-read and upload flow is clear in code and limited to one supplied file. It is risky for privacy, but there is no evidence of recursive scanning or hidden file collection.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Output Files Are Not Auto-Cleaned
medium_findings.description: Enhanced images are written to a temp imgs directory and the privacy documentation states they are not automatically cleaned. This can leave sensitive processed images on disk after the session ends.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temp output directory and file write are implemented in code, and the documentation explicitly says files are not auto-cleaned. The risk is local persistence, not covert exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings point to fenced bash examples that tell an agent how to run scripts/scan.py. These are executable instructions for the skill workflow, but they are not Ruby backticks or hidden shell execution in code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown examples and fixed command templates. They do execute a local Python script when followed, but I found no evidence of shell metacharacter construction or dynamic command execution in the Python implementation.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Alerts Are Benign Image Format Checks
low_findings.description: Hex-encoded byte sequences in file_saver.py are magic-byte constants for detecting PNG, JPEG, TIFF, and WBMP image formats. Base64 decoding is used to validate and save image data returned by the API.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The byte literals match standard image headers and are used in format detection before file saving. No evidence found that they decode executable code or hide network destinations.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-File Alerts Are Path Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and Credential Manager alerts appear to come from path traversal strings, markdown tables, or documentation text rather than code that reads those stores. I found no evidence that the skill accesses Windows SAM, Credential Manager, cookies, or unrelated hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code imports local modules, sets a fixed API URL, and documents excluded behaviors. The analyzer signals do not align with actual credential-store access in the inspected files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound POST with User Image Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The client builds a request containing either dataBase64 or dataUrl and posts it to the fixed Quark Scan King endpoint. This is expected, but it is the main data exposure pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request construction and outbound POST are explicit in code. The fixed endpoint makes intent clear, while the uploaded content can still be sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Local API Key Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The client reads SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY from the environment or a plaintext ~/.yescan_env file. This is a standard configuration pattern, but it requires local file permission controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code and documentation identify the key source and recommend permissions. This is not credential theft, but it is sensitive local storage.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Local Path Read
dangerous_patterns.description: The path workflow expands the supplied path, validates it, reads the file bytes, and converts them to base64 for upload. This should be limited to explicit user-approved images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads exactly the selected file path and enforces file type and size checks. No evidence found for broader filesystem traversal, but the selected file is transmitted.

content.user_title: Enhance Scanned Images with Quark Scan King
content.value_statement: Low quality document photos and screenshots are hard to read or reuse. This skill sends one approved image to Quark Scan King and returns a cleaner local image file.
actual_capabilities: Accepts one image per run as a URL, local file path, or base64 string.
Maps user intent to predefined scenes such as scan-document, remove-shadow, remove-watermark, and image-crop-rectify.
Calls the Quark Scan King API at a documented endpoint for image processing.
Decodes the returned image data and saves the enhanced file under the system temp directory.
Returns the saved image path or the upstream service error without adding extra processing.
limitations: Images and the API key are sent to a third-party service, so sensitive images need prior approval.
The skill requires Python 3 and a SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY environment variable or local key file.
It processes one static image at a time and documents a 5 MB input limit.
It is not intended for OCR extraction, Office conversion, video processing, or AI image generation.

use_cases.title: Clean Meeting Documents
use_cases.description: Improve a photographed page by reducing shadows, correcting the crop, and saving a clearer image for sharing.

use_cases.title: Prepare Study Materials
use_cases.description: Enhance photos of notes or exam papers so text is easier to read before reviewing or printing.

use_cases.title: Restore Design References
use_cases.description: Extract line art or convert a reference image into a cleaner sketch-style asset for further design work.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Image Cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a local file
prompt_templates.prompt: Enhance this document photo and save the cleaned image: /path/to/document.jpg

prompt_templates.title: Remove a Specific Defect
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with a named issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the shadows from this scanned page and keep the printed text readable: /path/to/page.png

prompt_templates.title: Rectify a Crooked Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for crop and perspective correction
prompt_templates.prompt: Crop and straighten this angled receipt photo so it looks like a flat scan: /path/to/receipt.jpg

prompt_templates.title: Select the Best Scene
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with quality and privacy constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: Choose the best supported enhancement scene for this image URL, process only this image, and return the saved output path.

output_examples.input: A blurry document photo is provided as a local file path.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a success message and a local temp path for the enhanced image.

output_examples.input: A screenshot has visible moire patterns and glare.
output_examples.output: The skill uses the screen-pattern removal scene and returns a cleaner saved image path.

output_examples.input: A receipt photo is skewed and includes extra desk background.
output_examples.output: The skill applies crop and rectification, then returns the corrected image location.

best_practices: Confirm that the image can be sent to Quark Scan King before processing sensitive content.
Use the most specific scene for the visible defect, such as remove-shadow or image-crop-rectify.
Delete temporary output files after they are no longer needed.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for confidential IDs, contracts, or medical images without explicit approval.
Do not pass entire folders or ask it to discover images automatically.
Do not use it when the task is text extraction, Office conversion, or generated image creation.

faq.question: Does this skill send images outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends the user-provided image to the Quark Scan King API for processing.

faq.question: Where are enhanced images saved?
faq.answer: They are saved under the system temp directory, usually in an imgs subdirectory.

faq.question: Does it read other files automatically?
faq.answer: No evidence found for directory scanning. It reads the single image path supplied for the current request.

faq.question: Can it process multiple images at once?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow processes one image per command.

faq.question: Does it extract text from images?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on image enhancement and cleanup, not OCR text extraction.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It requires SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY from the environment or from a local .yescan_env file.
